does: This paper introduces Honeyval, a systematic evaluation framework specifically designed for testing LLM-powered HTTP honeypots. The framework grounds honeypots in 16 real backend applications and employs AI hacking agents as attackers to simulate realistic threat scenarios. It incorporates two control tasks to monitor how agent and honeypot capabilities vary across different customizations, and establishes clear, verifiable exploit goals that attackers must achieve. This approach replaces ad-hoc evaluation methods with a scalable, reproducible methodology that can assess honeypot robustness across diverse configurations and attacker behaviors. The framework enables quantitative measurement of honeypot security properties beyond simple response similarity metrics.
connects: This work builds on the emerging intersection of large language models and cybersecurity, where recent research has explored using LLMs to generate realistic system responses for deception-based defenses. It extends prior honeypot research that traditionally relied on fixed rules or static datasets by incorporating modern AI agents as dynamic attackers. The paper responds to limitations in existing LLM-honeypot evaluation practices, which have relied on manual testing, deployment-based assessment, or response-level similarity matching. It also draws from the broader evaluation frameworks literature in adversarial AI, particularly work on benchmarking AI agents against interactive environments and measuring the effectiveness of game-playing or attacking agents in complex domains.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous development and deployment of LLM-powered honeypots by providing defenders with standardized evaluation tools adaptable to various configurations and threat models. It opens pathways for studying how honeypot characteristics and customizations affect their ability to deceive and contain attackers, potentially informing best practices for their design. The work could facilitate research into adversarial robustness of honeypots against increasingly sophisticated AI-driven attacks, and support the development of better defensive strategies through systematic analysis of failure modes. Additionally, the control task methodology could generalize to evaluating other interactive deception systems or security applications where agent-based testing is needed for realistic threat assessment.
